title: Follow-Up and Outcomes After Thermal Ablation Treatment Among HIV-Infected Women From Resource-Limited Countries With Positive HPV-Based Screening: Results of the AIMA-CC Cohort (ANRS 12375).
abstract: Primary screening for human papillomavirus (HPV) followed by treatment with thermal ablation (TA) is an important component of the global strategy to eliminate cervical cancer. However, evidence on post-TA outcomes in women living with HIV (WLWH) remains limited. The aim of this study is to evaluate the risk and determinants of post-TA CIN2+. As part of the AIMA-CC study, WLWH from Côte d'Ivoire, Burkina Faso and Cambodia were screened using HPV testing (GeneXpert). All HPV-positive women were biopsied and those with positive triage (positive visual inspection and/or HPV-161845) were treated with TA using a flat probe and followed for 12 months with HPV testing and biopsies. Among the 456 WLWH (age: 30-59 years) treated with TA and with valid histology, 396 (86%) attended the 12-month follow-up visit. HPV persistence (HPV of same GeneXpert channel at baseline and 12-month post-treatment) was 40% and was associated with baseline HPV16 positivity (p = 0.007), age ≥ 40 years (p = 0.03) and CD4 ≤ 500 cells/mm 3 (p = 0.02). CIN2+ was detected at 12 months in 12 (4%) of the 318 WLWH with valid histology and was associated with 12-month HPV positivity (OR: 5.29 [1.46-28.46]). The risk of CIN2+ was particularly high among women with HPV-16 at 12 months (OR: 15.53 [3.49-91.17]). HPV positivity at 12 months had a sensitivity for detecting CIN2+ of 83% but a specificity of 54%. HPV-16 positivity had lower sensitivity (42%) but high specificity (> 90%). In conclusion, there is an urgent need to improve TA management in WLWH who are at high risk of CIN2+ re-occurrence post-treatment.
